event.text: # Project Management Plan Update —

MAAP-1

**Program:** MAAP-1 Multi-Role Autonomous Aircraft Platform
**Document Type:** Program Management Plan (PMP) Update — Controlled Revision
**Change Authority:** Proposed Change — New Customer Order, 15
MAAP-1C "Atlas" Cargo Variant Aircraft, Deliveries Beginning 2029
**Revision Status:** DRAFT — Pending CCB Review

and Executive Approval
**Distribution:** Program Director, Deputy PM
, Chief Engineer, CFO, Supply Chain Director, Manufacturing Director, Contracts

Director, Risk Manager
**Document Basis:** Cross-functional specialized agent
analysis (cost/margin, risk/schedule, supply
chain, manufacturing, change impact); document-level impact assess
ments across maap1_integrated_master_schedule.md, maap1_risk_register.md, maap1_program_requirements
_baseline.md, maap1_financial_and_contract_management.
md, maap1_manufacturing_ramp_plan.md, maap1_supply_chain_procurement_plan.md, maap
1_bill_of_materials.md, maap1_customer_demand_forecast.md, and maap1_program_overview.md.

---

## 1. Introduction

### 1.1 Purpose of This Update

This Project Management Plan (PMP) Update is issued
in response to a proposed major programmatic change to the MAAP-1 program: a new customer order for fifteen (15) MAAP-1
C "Atlas" cargo variant aircraft with a stated delivery requirement beginning in 2029. This update supers
edes the relevant sections of the baseline PMP and constitutes the program's formal
, cross-functional response to the change proposal. It documents the impact assessment
findings, revises affected baselines, establishes new risk pos
ture and mitigation commitments, and provides the Program Director with a structured recommendation for contractual and programmatic disposition

.

This document is not a contract commitment. No
binding delivery obligations for 2029 should be executed until the conditions described

in Section 10 of this update are met and executive approval is obtained.

### 1.2 Background and

Context

The MAAP-1 program is currently in the Engineering and Manufacturing Development (EMD) phase.
The baseline Integrated Master Schedule (IMS) establishes Production Aircraft 1
(PA-1) rollout in April 2033, LR
IP authorization in November 2033, Initial Operating Capability (IOC) in July 2034, and Full
Rate Production (FRP) decision in September 2034. The production facility is certified for
a maximum throughput of two (2) aircraft per month at

FRP, equating to twenty-four (24) units per year.

Against this baseline
, the new customer's proposed delivery start of 2029 is struct
urally incompatible with the current IMS by approximately forty-eight (48) months. This incomp
atibility is the single most consequential finding of this analysis and drives the primary
recommendation of this PMP Update: the delivery schedule must be r
enegotiated before any contract is executed. The earliest credible first
delivery of a MAAP-1C under any reasonable acceleration scenario is
no sooner than 2033, with 2035–2036 being the realistic
contractual delivery window aligned to the FRP authorization and r

amp.

Notwithstanding the schedule conflict, the order itself represents a material
commercial opportunity. Fifteen additional aircraft at FRP-era
pricing represent approximately $2.2–2.4 billion in new contract
revenue and approximately $120–345 million in gross margin contribution, depending
on contract pricing discipline and execution performance. The program's financial architecture —
learning curve economics, NRC amortization over a
larger production base, and fixed overhead absorption — improves with additional production
volume. The order is worth pursuing. It must

simply be pursued on terms that are consistent with the program's production readiness timeline.

### 1.3

Document Scope and Limitations

This PMP Update addresses impacts across production schedule
, supply chain and procurement, manufacturing, program financials, risk posture, quality management, workforce
planning, and configuration control. It is based on the best available program data as
documented in the referenced baseline documents. Areas where data is incomplete or where
the cargo variant configuration has not been formally defined — most notably the unresolved CBD
-0014 cargo hook load rating and the absence
of a MAAP-1C Bill of Materials (BOM) section — are explicitly flagged as blocking items

requiring resolution before downstream planning can be finalized.

This document does not replace
the need for a formal CCB change action, an independent
cost estimate for the cargo variant, or a negotiated contract. It provides

the analytic and programmatic foundation for those activities.

---

## 2.

Project Scope

### 2.1 Scope of the Proposed Change

The proposed change introduces the following new scope to the MAAP-1 program:

**Production
Scope:** Fifteen (15) production aircraft of the MAAP-1C "Atlas" cargo variant configuration for a new
commercial or government customer. This represents a 15% increase in the current
production program quantity (from 100 to 115 aircraft), extending the total

production run and improving per-aircraft NRC amortization economics.

**Variant Configuration
Scope:** The MAAP-1C is an established variant within the program architecture
, sharing the Green Aircraft baseline (airframe primary structure
, rotor system, engines, FADEC, fly-by-wire flight
controls, avionics bus, and electrical architecture) with the MAAP-1F
and MAAP-1I variants. The MAAP-1C variant-unique systems
include: dual cargo hook assemblies (rated to 10
,000 lb threshold / 12,000 lb objective per
KPP-03, with 16
,000 lb demonstrated in Phase 6B flight testing
); cargo rail and roller restraint system (463
L-compatible); load weight indication and proximity
alert systems; cargo bay side wall and
access port assemblies; aft cargo ramp and clam
-shell door assembly with hydraulic actuators; cargo
management and load planning software; and external load stability
augmentation (sling release and pendulum angle
monitoring). Optional rescue hoist capability is defined

in the drawing tree.

**New BOM Scope:** The MAAP-1C variant currently
has no dedicated BOM section. Group 17 (Special Mission Equipment) does
not include a CG-1 cargo variant subsection, and no
variant-specific UPUC exists in Section 14
.2 of the BOM document. Creation of a complete MAAP-1C BOM delta —
identifying all variant-unique items, their make/buy designations, lead times, part
numbers, and unit costs — is a critical prerequisite for all downstream procurement, production, and financial

planning.

**Configuration Control Scope:** CBD-0014 (cargo hook load rating) remains open. ICD
-004 (external hardpoint interface) cannot be finalized until CBD-0014
is resolved. This open item blocks production drawing release for
MAAP-1C-specific hardware and must be closed through a formal CCB action before the

contract is executed.

**New Customer Scope:** The customer identity has not been specified
in the change proposal. Customer classification — U.S. Do
D entity, Foreign Military Sales (FMS) partner, or commercial operator
— determines the applicable regulatory pathway (Army Contracting Command
vehicle, DSCA/State Department approval, FAA-
certificated delivery), pricing tier, and contract mechanism. This

determination must be made before contract structure can be finalized.

**Autonomous Capability Scope:** KPP-07 establ
ishes a fully autonomous (zero-pilot) objective for the MAAP-1C in
permissive airspace. If the new customer requires autonomous operational capability, delivery
is contingent upon FAA type certification inclusive of autonomous modes
— a currently Critical risk (RISK-CR-001
, Score 16). The contract must explicitly address
which autonomy level will be certified and delivered at initial acceptance

.

### 2.2 Scope Exclusions

The following are explicitly excluded from this change's scope unless a separate

CCB action is initiated:

- Any modification to the Green Aircraft structural load paths

, primary wiring architecture, or fly-by-wire flight control software
- Any customer-unique
requirements that deviate from the standard MAAP-1C configuration as defined in the PRB
— any such deviations constitute a Class I modification,
priced at 18–20% margin and funded by the

customer
- New airworthiness type certification activities — the MAAP-1C is an
existing variant within the current certification basis; additional production

aircraft of the same configuration do not require new type certification
- Logistics and
sustainment infrastructure specific to the new customer's operating environment, unless

separately contracted

### 2.3 Variant Architecture Integrity

The addition of
15 MAAP-1C aircraft does not require modification to the program's modular open systems
architecture (MOSA) or the six interface control documents (ICD-001 through ICD-006)
governing the Green Aircraft / mission equipment interface, provided
no deviations from the standard MAAP-1C configuration are introduced. The 80%+ common
ality metric (PRB Section 6.2) is expected to be sustained
or improved by the expanded MAAP-1C production run, given the cargo variant's mission
equipment costs are lower than combat or ISR variants
. The commonality metric will be formally recalculated and reported
at the next Program Management Review (PMR) following CC

B approval.

---

## 3. Milestone List & Schedule Baseline

### 3.1 Baseline Schedule Conflict — Executive

Summary

The single most critical finding of this PMP Update is that the customer-proposed
2029 delivery start is **not achievable under the current IMS baseline**. The IMS establishes
that no production aircraft can be delivered before approximately Q4 2033 under any credible scenario
. The 2029 delivery commitment predates the following mandatory milestones by the margins

shown:

| Mandatory Baseline Milestone | IMS Date | Gap to

2029 Delivery |
|---|---|---|
| PA-1 First Production Aircraft Rollout | April 2033 | ~

48 months |
| PA-1 First Flight | June 2033 | ~48 months |
| Production Process Validated

| November 2033 | ~54 months |
| LRIP Authorization (Lot 1) | November 2033 | ~54 months |
| IOC

Achieved | July 2034 | ~60 months |
| FRP Decision | September 2034 | ~62 months |

Comm
itting to 2029 deliveries without renegotiating the schedule exposes the program to near
-certain liquidated damages liability. At the documented rate of $8
M in LD per major schedule event
, a 48-month delivery miss would generate damages that could eliminate the entire
revenue value of the order. This exposure is categorized as a Critical Risk (NEW-
01, Score 16) in Section 6

of this Update.

### 3.2 Negotiated Delivery Schedule — Recommended Position

The program's recommended contractual position for delivery schedule negotiations is as follows:

| Delivery

Period | Recommended Units | Basis |
|---|---|---|
| 2029 | 0 (or
1–2 maximum if customer demands symbolic delivery) | Pre-

LRIP authorization; not producible |
| 2030–2032 | 0 (
LRIP lot reserved for baseline customer) | LRIP process validation priority

|
| 2033 | 2–3 (earliest feasible, if FRP pulled

left) | Post-PA-1 first flight; LRIP Lot 1 |
| 2034 | 5–6 |

FRP-authorized production |
| 2035 | 5–6 | FRP steady state;

15-unit total delivered |
| 2036 | Remainder

(if applicable) | FRP sustained production |

If the customer insists on earlier symbolic deliveries, a
maximum of 1–2 aircraft in 2033 may be structured as a demonstration
or early-delivery milestone unit, provided LRIP authorization has
been granted and FAA type certification (with applicable autonomous modes) is confirmed. Any delivery before
LRIP authorization must be treated as a contractual risk and individually approved by the Program

Director and CFO.

The financial model should structure milestone
payments around production events — contract award, long
-lead procurement completion, LRIP authorization, first production acceptance — rather than calendar
dates, to protect both parties from the consequences of regulatory or developmental

delays beyond program control.

### 3.4 Impact

on Existing IMS Milestones

The following existing IMS milestones are impacted and must be formally

assessed in the CCB change action:

- **LRIP Lot 3 (2
029, 23 units):** The customer demand forecast documents
this lot at 23 baseline units. Adding 15 cargo variant aircraft creates a combined
2029 demand of 38 units — 79% of authorized annual
FRP capacity in the first FRP-qualifying year. This is not
producible at the baselined ramp rate and requires either delivery schedule restruct
uring (recommended) or a formal capacity expansion decision with capital funding

approval.
- **FRP-2 Steady State (2030,
48 units/year):** The FRP-2 ceiling was planned for the baseline
demand profile. Adding 15 cargo units to the extended production run does not breach the FRP-2 ceiling
if deliveries are distributed across 2034–2036, but comp
resses available buffer and eliminates headroom for additional opportun

istic orders in that period.
- **Production Drawing Release (April 2032
):** The MAAP-1C production drawings cannot be finalized until CBD-0014 is closed and ICD-004 is base
lined. If CBD-0014 closure is delayed past Q3 2027, the April 2032 production

drawing release milestone is at risk for cargo-variant-specific drawings.
- **Air
worthiness Certification (January 2032):** This is a hard external dependency. No cargo
variant delivery can be contractually committed before this milestone is achieved. The cargo hook
structural certification data package must be formally incorporated into the type certificate

as part of this milestone.

### 3.5 Schedule Reserve Assessment
The existing IMS carries a 30-day management reserve at program level
and 6-week schedule reserve per FRP phase. These
reserves were sized for single-variant, steady-state production risks
. The introduction of a concurrent new variant — with its
own tooling development, supply chain qualification, assembly flow design, and production learning curve — substantially
exceeds the risk environment these reserves were sized to absorb. Section
6 of this Update establishes a revised risk register with associated schedule exposure.
The Program Director should request a Monte Carlo re-simulation of the IMS incorporating two
-variant production concurrency before finalizing the negoti

ated delivery schedule with the customer.

---

## 4. Change Management Plan

### 4.1 Change Classification

This proposed change is classified as a **Class
I Major Programmatic Change** under the program's change management framework. Classification criteria met
include: new contract with a new customer, introduction
of cargo variant production scope not previously baselined in the IMS, capital plan
amendment required, supply chain contract actions required, EVM
baseline re-planning required, and potential impact to type certification scope

.

Per the contract modification framework, Class I changes carry 18
–20% margin authorization. This precedent must be applied to the pricing structure
of the new cargo variant contract: the variant NR
E, learning curve uncertainty on cargo-unique systems
, and supply chain risk premium collectively justify pricing at a
minimum of 13–15% gross margin, with 15% as the recommended
floor. Under no circumstances should this contract be priced at baseline fighter

variant rates.

### 4.2 Change Control Board Actions Required

The following

formal CCB actions are required and must be initiated within 30 days of this PMP Update approval:

**Action 1
— CBD-0014 Resolution:** Close the open configuration baseline deviation
for cargo hook load rating. The T&E program (
Phase 6B) has demonstrated 16,000 lb external load
capability, providing the technical basis. A CCB action against
ICD-004 is required to formally baseline the hook rating. This is a blocking

item for production drawing release, supply chain qualification, and contract execution.

**Action 2 — MAAP-1
C Production Scope Addition:** Formally add 15-aircraft MAAP-1C production scope to the program
EVM baseline, the IMS, and the manufacturing ramp plan as
a controlled scope change. The EVM Baseline Change Proposal (BCP) must

be submitted within 30 days of contract execution.

**Action 3 — BOM Amendment:** Authorize
the creation of Group 17, Section 17.1.5
(Cargo Variant CG-1 Equipment) in the BOM document, with drawing
reference AF-MAAP1-SM-5000-00
. All cargo-variant-specific items must be assigned part numbers, make
/buy designations, lead time classifications, and unit costs in this BOM section before procurement actions

are initiated.

**Action 4 — NRC Amortization Re-baselining:** Update Section
14.3 of the BOM to reflect the revised production base of 115 aircraft (from 100).
The per-aircraft NRC burden decreases from $7,938,
150 to approximately $6,903,609 — a $1,034
,541 reduction per aircraft across the full program. This saving must be reflected in the program unit cost table

(Section 14.4) and formally reported at the next PMR.

**Action 5
— IMS WBS Extension:** Add dedicated cargo
variant development WBS elements including: cargo variant requirements
and interface control, MAAP-1C tooling and fixture development
, cargo-specific supplier qualification and long-lead procurement sub-schedule, cargo

variant assembly flow design, and cargo-specific certification data package development.

### 4.3 Impact

Assessment Across All Baseline Documents

Each baseline document has been assessed for impact. The following table

summarizes required updates, priority, and responsible owner:

| Required Actions | Priority | Owner |
|---|---|---|---|---|

MAAP-1C WBS; revise Lot 3; add cargo mil

8 new risks; elevate 6 existing risks; revise EM

V calculations | Immediate | Risk Manager |

17 CG-1 section; update Section 14.2, 14.3, 14.4; flag

LL items | Within 60 days | Chief Engineer |
| maap1_manufacturing_ramp_plan.md
| HIGH | Add cargo variant phases; revise capital plan; update supplier capacity tables;

revise exit strategy | Within 60 days | Manufacturing Director |

IL; update MRP parameters; initiate LTA amendment;

add new risks | Within 30 days | Supply Chain Director |

Revise Lot 3 demand; update variant mix; revise Monte Carlo inputs; update buffer

strategy | Within 60 days | Program Manager |

financial model; update scenario planning; revise supplier risk EMV | Within 60 days

| CFO |

PP-07 autonomy lot applicability; update commonality metric

update market section; add risk section; clarify pricing basis

### 4.4 Requirements Freeze and Change Control Discipline
One of the most significant margin protection mechanisms available to the program is a hard requirements freeze for the
MAAP-1C configuration at contract signature. Any post-signature customer
requirement changes — modifications to hook rating, cargo capacity, av
ionics interfaces, autonomy level, or support provisions — must be classified as Class I modifications
and priced at 18–20% margin, funded by the customer. The Change Control Board must enforce this discipline

without exception.

The PRB's existing framework (Section 5.1
) requires Chief Engineer and PEO waiver for any deviation
from the common configuration boundary. This governance process provides the contract
ual and technical authority to enforce the requirements freeze. The contracts team must ensure
the customer agreement explicitly references the CCB process and

Class I modification pricing before execution.

Any new customer requirements that deviate from the standard MAAP-1C specification — hook
spacing differences, unique avionics interfaces, special cargo configurations, customer
-specific software — erode the commonality metric (PRB Section 6.2) and trigger an
impact assessment against all three variants per PRB Section 5
.3. Commonality erosion below 80% requires formal PMR reporting

and corrective action.

---

## 5. Cost Management Plan & Cost Baseline

### 5.1 Financial Opportunity Summary
The 15-unit MAAP-1C order represents a material financial opportunity for the program. The following

table summarizes the revenue and margin scenario analysis:

| Scenario | Revenue per Unit

| 15-Unit Revenue | Total Cost | Gross Margin | Margin % |
|---|---|---|---|

---|---|
| **Optimistic** (FRP pricing, mature learning
curve, disciplined NRE recovery) | ~$155M

.8% |
| **Base Case** (LRIP-era pricing, partial learning curve benefit

% |
| **Pessimistic** (cost growth, learning curve slip, supply chain premium) | ~$148
pessimistic scenario represents a loss position.** It is achievable through a
combination of baseline-rate pricing, cargo variant learning curve underperformance, and supply chain cost growth
— all of which are individually plausible given current program conditions. The program must not enter
this contract without pricing discipline. The recommended minimum
gross margin floor is **13–15%**, with 15% as the negotiating

target.

### 5.2 Cargo Variant Cost Structure

A cargo variant-specific BOM does
not yet exist. Until Group 17 Section CG-1 is completed, all cost estimates
carry significant uncertainty. The following working estimates are established for program planning
purposes, pending the Independent Cost Estimate (ICE) directed in Section 4.3

:

| Cost Element | Low Estimate | High Estimate | Basis |
|---|---|---|---|
| Recurring airframe and
systems (Green Aircraft baseline) | $118M | $125
M per aircraft | Baseline minus armament/targeting (~$16.
5M Group 12), plus cargo structural reinforcement |
| Variant-unique mission equipment (cargo systems) | $3M | $6
M per aircraft | Cargo rail/roller, hooks, ramp, load monitoring

|
| Program management and QA burden (estimated
12%) | $14.5M | $15.7M per aircraft | Proportional to recurring

cost |
| **Estimated MAAP-1C Flyaway Cost (per aircraft)** | **~

$135M** | **~$147M** | Working estimate only |
| Variant N
RE (cargo delta design, tooling, qualification) | $25
M | $45M | One-time; to be recovered in contract pricing

|
| Supply chain re-qualification and LLI commitments | $15

M | $30M | Cargo-specific supplier actions |
| Production rate disruption premium
| $20M | $50M | For any schedule acceleration from F

RP baseline |
| **Total Program Cost Addition (15 aircraft)** | **~$2,

065M** | **~$2,275M** | Program total |

**Learning curve treatment:** The program's
85% cumulative learning curve on vertically integrated systems cannot be fully applied
to the MAAP-1C. Approximately 70% of the cargo variant cost (Green Aircraft systems
) inherits the existing fighter-variant learning curve and benefits from production mat
urity. The remaining 30% (cargo-unique systems) resets to an early
-curve position for cargo-variant builds. Conserv
atively modeled at a 90% curve for cargo-unique
systems (vs. 85% for common systems), the cost impact on 15
units is approximately $18–28M above the optimistic estimate.
This risk must be priced into the contract and tracked as a standalone

cost metric beginning with the first MAAP-1C production unit.

### 5.3 N

RC Amortization Improvement

The increase in production base from 100 to 115 aircraft reduces the per-aircraft NRC burden from $7,938,150 to
approximately $6,903,609 — a saving of approximately $1.03M per aircraft across all
115 production units. The total program NRC saving attributable to the expanded
production base is approximately $15.5M (15 units × ~$1.03M per unit) in reduced
amortization cost for the original 100-aircraft program. This is a genuine

program-level financial benefit that partially offsets the cargo variant integration costs.

total does not include cargo variant-specific investments. The following incremental capital requirements are established:

jig, door fixture, hook test fixture,

## 6. Risk Management Plan & Risk Register

### 6.1 Risk Management Approach

The cargo variant order introduces a new risk tier to the
MAAP-1 program. Previously, program risks were scoped to a single-variant, single-customer
production program advancing through EMD. This change adds
concurrent variant production planning, a new customer relationship, a
new supply chain thread, new tooling requirements, and a delivery commitment that is
structurally misaligned with the current IMS. The risk management approach for

this change is:

- **Risk separation:** Cargo variant risks are tracked in a
dedicated risk register appendix and managed with separate mitigation funding.

Baseline program risk reserve ($74M) is ring-fenced.
- **Risk authority:** The Program Director retains approval
authority for any risk mitigation expenditure above $1M. Risks rated Critical

(Score 16) require Program Director and CFO co-approval for mitigation funding.
- **Risk
cadence:** Cargo variant risks are reviewed at the standing Monthly Program Review (PMR) under
a new agenda item: *Cargo Variant Integration Status*. Critical
risks receive weekly status at the Program Director's discret

ion.
- **Risk metrics:** Risk score = Likelihood ×
Consequence, 1–4 scale each,
maximum Score 16. All new risks are scored in this section
. Scores of 12–16 are rated High or Critical and

require active mitigation plans.

## 8. Quality Management Plan

### 8.1 Quality Risk

from Variant Introduction

The introduction of a new variant configuration
during the FRP-1/FRP-2 transition period is a well-documented quality risk in
analogous programs (F-35 multi-variant production, CH
-47 variant introductions). Historical data from comparable programs shows First Time
Quality (FTQ) degradation of 3–8% during variant
introduction periods. The program's FTQ targets — 97
(FRP-2) — are at risk for the cargo variant production stream

during its first 8–12 production units.

The quality
management plan's existing "Rate Transition Management
" protocol must be formally extended to cover variant introduction, not
only rate increases. The protocol should include: enhanced quality surveillance
for all cargo-unique assembly operations; daily production quality
reviews for the first 12 cargo variant aircraft; a dedicated rapid
response quality team assigned to the cargo production stream; and formal first article inspection (
FAI) per AS9102 before the first cargo production unit is

released to the line.

### 8.2 Cargo Variant First Article Inspection

A dedicated
MAAP-1C First Article Inspection (FAI) per AS9102B is required before production units are released to the assembly
line. The FAI scope must cover all cargo-variant-unique systems

and interfaces, including:

- Cargo hook structural interface and load path (

ICD-004 compliance verification)
- Cargo floor structure dimensional inspection (titan

ium longeron assembly)
- Cargo ramp mechanism kinematic verification (full

cycle, hydraulic pressure)
- Cargo rail and restra

int system load verification (463L compatibility)
- Cargo bay side wall and access port fit-up tolerance verification
- Weight

and balance measurement (cargo configuration C.G. envelope)
- Cargo-

unique wiring and avionics integration functional check

The FAI must be completed and formally closed
before the second cargo unit enters the assembly line. Any FAI findings
that require production drawing changes must be processed through the CCB and may

impact the production drawing release milestone.

### 8.3 Special Process

Qualification — Cargo-Unique Requirements

The cargo variant introduces materials
and processes not currently in the Qualified Special Processes list at the
scale required for production. The following special process qualifications must be completed before first cargo variant

production unit:

**Titanium Structure NDT:** The cargo floor
primary structure uses titanium alloy (AMS 4928 equivalent). The current
NDT program covers titanium welding (AWS D17.1)
and standard ASNT SNT-TC-1A/NAS-410 inspection. Titanium machined
primary structure at the dimensional scale of the floor lon
gerons requires specific ultrasonic inspection qualification that must be developed and validated. ND
T procedures for the cargo hook structural attachment (
10,000 lb load path) require specialized ultrasonic or radiographic inspection techniques

not currently documented in the production quality plan.

**Titanium Machining Process Qualification:** If
the titanium floor longerons are produced in-house (MAKE decision), the machining process for
AMS 4928 at the required dimensional tolerances must be formally qualified. Titanium machining is a controlled
process with specific cutting parameters, coolant requirements, and tool inspection protocols. Process

qualification documentation must be completed before production.

**Cargo Ramp Hydraulic System Leak and Functional
Test:** The cargo ramp hydraulic system requires a production acceptance test procedure (ATP) that does
not exist in the current test flow. The test must verify full-cycle ramp operation,
hydraulic pressure at rated load, leak test per MIL-STD-810
, and emergency release function. A dedicated test stand is required (estimated $

3–5M, included in capital plan amendment).

**Cargo Hook
Load Test:** The primary cargo hook assembly requires a proof load test to rated capacity (minimum
10,000 lb threshold) on each production unit before acceptance
. A structural test fixture must be designed and fabricated. The test procedure
must be incorporated into the production acceptance test flow, with results documented in the aircraft

acceptance data package.

### 8.4 Cargo Variant Acceptance Testing

The customer acceptance
process must be extended for the MAAP-1C to include the

following cargo-specific acceptance tests:

- Cargo hook proof load test (threshold: 10,000 lb
; objective: 12,000 lb per KPP-03
- External load stability augmentation software functional check (s
ling release, pendulum angle monitoring, load weight

indication)
- Cargo weight and balance verification (C.G. at maximum cargo loading)
- Cargo-unique

avionics display and crew alerting functional test

A dedicated Cargo Acceptance Test Procedure (CATP) document
must be developed and approved before the first cargo production unit enters final assembly. This is a documentation
critical path item whose development must begin conc

urrently with assembly flow design.

### 8.5 Quality Hold Policy — Cargo Variant First

Articles

The baseline quality plan mandates a 90-day hold on the first three
(3) production aircraft for quality assessment. This policy
must be explicitly extended to the first three (3) MAAP-1C cargo production units,
regardless of their position in the overall production sequence. The $
2M retrofit reserve fund was sized for the baseline variant
; an additional cargo-specific retrofit reserve of $1–2M should

# Project Management Plan Update — MAAP-1

**Program:** MAAP-1 Multi-Role Autonomous Aircraft Platform
**Document Type:** Program Management Plan (PMP) Update — Controlled Revision
**Change Authority:** Proposed Change — New Customer Order, 15 MAAP-1C "Atlas" Cargo Variant Aircraft, Deliveries Beginning 2029
**Revision Status:** DRAFT — Pending CCB Review and Executive Approval
**Distribution:** Program Director, Deputy PM, Chief Engineer, CFO, Supply Chain Director, Manufacturing Director, Contracts Director, Risk Manager
**Document Basis:** Cross-functional specialized agent analysis (cost/margin, risk/schedule, supply chain, manufacturing, change impact); document-level impact assessments across maap1_integrated_master_schedule.md, maap1_risk_register.md, maap1_program_requirements_baseline.md, maap1_financial_and_contract_management.md, maap1_manufacturing_ramp_plan.md, maap1_supply_chain_procurement_plan.md, maap1_bill_of_materials.md, maap1_customer_demand_forecast.md, and maap1_program_overview.md.

---

## 1. Introduction

### 1.1 Purpose of This Update

This Project Management Plan (PMP) Update is issued in response to a proposed major programmatic change to the MAAP-1 program: a new customer order for fifteen (15) MAAP-1C "Atlas" cargo variant aircraft with a stated delivery requirement beginning in 2029. This update supersedes the relevant sections of the baseline PMP and constitutes the program's formal, cross-functional response to the change proposal. It documents the impact assessment findings, revises affected baselines, establishes new risk posture and mitigation commitments, and provides the Program Director with a structured recommendation for contractual and programmatic disposition.

This document is not a contract commitment. No binding delivery obligations for 2029 should be executed until the conditions described in Section 10 of this update are met and executive approval is obtained.

### 1.2 Background and Context

The MAAP-1 program is currently in the Engineering and Manufacturing Development (EMD) phase. The baseline Integrated Master Schedule (IMS) establishes Production Aircraft 1 (PA-1) rollout in April 2033, LRIP authorization in November 2033, Initial Operating Capability (IOC) in July 2034, and Full Rate Production (FRP) decision in September 2034. The production facility is certified for a maximum throughput of two (2) aircraft per month at FRP, equating to twenty-four (24) units per year.

Against this baseline, the new customer's proposed delivery start of 2029 is structurally incompatible with the current IMS by approximately forty-eight (48) months. This incompatibility is the single most consequential finding of this analysis and drives the primary recommendation of this PMP Update: the delivery schedule must be renegotiated before any contract is executed. The earliest credible first delivery of a MAAP-1C under any reasonable acceleration scenario is no sooner than 2033, with 2035–2036 being the realistic contractual delivery window aligned to the FRP authorization and ramp.

Notwithstanding the schedule conflict, the order itself represents a material commercial opportunity. Fifteen additional aircraft at FRP-era pricing represent approximately $2.2–2.4 billion in new contract revenue and approximately $120–345 million in gross margin contribution, depending on contract pricing discipline and execution performance. The program's financial architecture — learning curve economics, NRC amortization over a larger production base, and fixed overhead absorption — improves with additional production volume. The order is worth pursuing. It must simply be pursued on terms that are consistent with the program's production readiness timeline.

### 1.3 Document Scope and Limitations

This PMP Update addresses impacts across production schedule, supply chain and procurement, manufacturing, program financials, risk posture, quality management, workforce planning, and configuration control. It is based on the best available program data as documented in the referenced baseline documents. Areas where data is incomplete or where the cargo variant configuration has not been formally defined — most notably the unresolved CBD-0014 cargo hook load rating and the absence of a MAAP-1C Bill of Materials (BOM) section — are explicitly flagged as blocking items requiring resolution before downstream planning can be finalized.

This document does not replace the need for a formal CCB change action, an independent cost estimate for the cargo variant, or a negotiated contract. It provides the analytic and programmatic foundation for those activities.

---

## 2. Project Scope

### 2.1 Scope of the Proposed Change

The proposed change introduces the following new scope to the MAAP-1 program:

**Production Scope:** Fifteen (15) production aircraft of the MAAP-1C "Atlas" cargo variant configuration for a new commercial or government customer. This represents a 15% increase in the current production program quantity (from 100 to 115 aircraft), extending the total production run and improving per-aircraft NRC amortization economics.

**Variant Configuration Scope:** The MAAP-1C is an established variant within the program architecture, sharing the Green Aircraft baseline (airframe primary structure, rotor system, engines, FADEC, fly-by-wire flight controls, avionics bus, and electrical architecture) with the MAAP-1F and MAAP-1I variants. The MAAP-1C variant-unique systems include: dual cargo hook assemblies (rated to 10,000 lb threshold / 12,000 lb objective per KPP-03, with 16,000 lb demonstrated in Phase 6B flight testing); cargo rail and roller restraint system (463L-compatible); load weight indication and proximity alert systems; cargo bay side wall and access port assemblies; aft cargo ramp and clam-shell door assembly with hydraulic actuators; cargo management and load planning software; and external load stability augmentation (sling release and pendulum angle monitoring). Optional rescue hoist capability is defined in the drawing tree.

**New BOM Scope:** The MAAP-1C variant currently has no dedicated BOM section. Group 17 (Special Mission Equipment) does not include a CG-1 cargo variant subsection, and no variant-specific UPUC exists in Section 14.2 of the BOM document. Creation of a complete MAAP-1C BOM delta — identifying all variant-unique items, their make/buy designations, lead times, part numbers, and unit costs — is a critical prerequisite for all downstream procurement, production, and financial planning.

**Configuration Control Scope:** CBD-0014 (cargo hook load rating) remains open. ICD-004 (external hardpoint interface) cannot be finalized until CBD-0014 is resolved. This open item blocks production drawing release for MAAP-1C-specific hardware and must be closed through a formal CCB action before the contract is executed.

**New Customer Scope:** The customer identity has not been specified in the change proposal. Customer classification — U.S. DoD entity, Foreign Military Sales (FMS) partner, or commercial operator — determines the applicable regulatory pathway (Army Contracting Command vehicle, DSCA/State Department approval, FAA-certificated delivery), pricing tier, and contract mechanism. This determination must be made before contract structure can be finalized.

**Autonomous Capability Scope:** KPP-07 establishes a fully autonomous (zero-pilot) objective for the MAAP-1C in permissive airspace. If the new customer requires autonomous operational capability, delivery is contingent upon FAA type certification inclusive of autonomous modes — a currently Critical risk (RISK-CR-001, Score 16). The contract must explicitly address which autonomy level will be certified and delivered at initial acceptance.

### 2.2 Scope Exclusions

The following are explicitly excluded from this change's scope unless a separate CCB action is initiated:

- Any modification to the Green Aircraft structural load paths, primary wiring architecture, or fly-by-wire flight control software
- Any customer-unique requirements that deviate from the standard MAAP-1C configuration as defined in the PRB — any such deviations constitute a Class I modification, priced at 18–20% margin and funded by the customer
- New airworthiness type certification activities — the MAAP-1C is an existing variant within the current certification basis; additional production aircraft of the same configuration do not require new type certification
- Logistics and sustainment infrastructure specific to the new customer's operating environment, unless separately contracted

### 2.3 Variant Architecture Integrity

The addition of 15 MAAP-1C aircraft does not require modification to the program's modular open systems architecture (MOSA) or the six interface control documents (ICD-001 through ICD-006) governing the Green Aircraft / mission equipment interface, provided no deviations from the standard MAAP-1C configuration are introduced. The 80%+ commonality metric (PRB Section 6.2) is expected to be sustained or improved by the expanded MAAP-1C production run, given the cargo variant's mission equipment costs are lower than combat or ISR variants. The commonality metric will be formally recalculated and reported at the next Program Management Review (PMR) following CCB approval.

---

## 3. Milestone List & Schedule Baseline

### 3.1 Baseline Schedule Conflict — Executive Summary

The single most critical finding of this PMP Update is that the customer-proposed 2029 delivery start is **not achievable under the current IMS baseline**. The IMS establishes that no production aircraft can be delivered before approximately Q4 2033 under any credible scenario. The 2029 delivery commitment predates the following mandatory milestones by the margins shown:

| Mandatory Baseline Milestone | IMS Date | Gap to 2029 Delivery |
|---|---|---|
| PA-1 First Production Aircraft Rollout | April 2033 | ~48 months |
| PA-1 First Flight | June 2033 | ~48 months |
| Production Process Validated | November 2033 | ~54 months |
| LRIP Authorization (Lot 1) | November 2033 | ~54 months |
| IOC Achieved | July 2034 | ~60 months |
| FRP Decision | September 2034 | ~62 months |

Committing to 2029 deliveries without renegotiating the schedule exposes the program to near-certain liquidated damages liability. At the documented rate of $8M in LD per major schedule event, a 48-month delivery miss would generate damages that could eliminate the entire revenue value of the order. This exposure is categorized as a Critical Risk (NEW-01, Score 16) in Section 6 of this Update.

### 3.2 Negotiated Delivery Schedule — Recommended Position

The program's recommended contractual position for delivery schedule negotiations is as follows:

| Delivery Period | Recommended Units | Basis |
|---|---|---|
| 2029 | 0 (or 1–2 maximum if customer demands symbolic delivery) | Pre-LRIP authorization; not producible |
| 2030–2032 | 0 (LRIP lot reserved for baseline customer) | LRIP process validation priority |
| 2033 | 2–3 (earliest feasible, if FRP pulled left) | Post-PA-1 first flight; LRIP Lot 1 |
| 2034 | 5–6 | FRP-authorized production |
| 2035 | 5–6 | FRP steady state; 15-unit total delivered |
| 2036 | Remainder (if applicable) | FRP sustained production |

If the customer insists on earlier symbolic deliveries, a maximum of 1–2 aircraft in 2033 may be structured as a demonstration or early-delivery milestone unit, provided LRIP authorization has been granted and FAA type certification (with applicable autonomous modes) is confirmed. Any delivery before LRIP authorization must be treated as a contractual risk and individually approved by the Program Director and CFO.

The financial model should structure milestone payments around production events — contract award, long-lead procurement completion, LRIP authorization, first production acceptance — rather than calendar dates, to protect both parties from the consequences of regulatory or developmental delays beyond program control.

### 3.3 Revised Milestone Schedule — Cargo Variant Development Track

The following milestones must be added to the IMS as a dedicated MAAP-1C Production Track. These are additions to the existing IMS structure, not replacements:

### 3.4 Impact on Existing IMS Milestones

The following existing IMS milestones are impacted and must be formally assessed in the CCB change action:

- **LRIP Lot 3 (2029, 23 units):** The customer demand forecast documents this lot at 23 baseline units. Adding 15 cargo variant aircraft creates a combined 2029 demand of 38 units — 79% of authorized annual FRP capacity in the first FRP-qualifying year. This is not producible at the baselined ramp rate and requires either delivery schedule restructuring (recommended) or a formal capacity expansion decision with capital funding approval.
- **FRP-2 Steady State (2030, 48 units/year):** The FRP-2 ceiling was planned for the baseline demand profile. Adding 15 cargo units to the extended production run does not breach the FRP-2 ceiling if deliveries are distributed across 2034–2036, but compresses available buffer and eliminates headroom for additional opportunistic orders in that period.
- **Production Drawing Release (April 2032):** The MAAP-1C production drawings cannot be finalized until CBD-0014 is closed and ICD-004 is baselined. If CBD-0014 closure is delayed past Q3 2027, the April 2032 production drawing release milestone is at risk for cargo-variant-specific drawings.
- **Airworthiness Certification (January 2032):** This is a hard external dependency. No cargo variant delivery can be contractually committed before this milestone is achieved. The cargo hook structural certification data package must be formally incorporated into the type certificate as part of this milestone.

### 3.5 Schedule Reserve Assessment

The existing IMS carries a 30-day management reserve at program level and 6-week schedule reserve per FRP phase. These reserves were sized for single-variant, steady-state production risks. The introduction of a concurrent new variant — with its own tooling development, supply chain qualification, assembly flow design, and production learning curve — substantially exceeds the risk environment these reserves were sized to absorb. Section 6 of this Update establishes a revised risk register with associated schedule exposure. The Program Director should request a Monte Carlo re-simulation of the IMS incorporating two-variant production concurrency before finalizing the negotiated delivery schedule with the customer.

## 4. Change Management Plan

### 4.1 Change Classification

This proposed change is classified as a **Class I Major Programmatic Change** under the program's change management framework. Classification criteria met include: new contract with a new customer, introduction of cargo variant production scope not previously baselined in the IMS, capital plan amendment required, supply chain contract actions required, EVM baseline re-planning required, and potential impact to type certification scope.

Per the contract modification framework, Class I changes carry 18–20% margin authorization. This precedent must be applied to the pricing structure of the new cargo variant contract: the variant NRE, learning curve uncertainty on cargo-unique systems, and supply chain risk premium collectively justify pricing at a minimum of 13–15% gross margin, with 15% as the recommended floor. Under no circumstances should this contract be priced at baseline fighter variant rates.

### 4.2 Change Control Board Actions Required

The following formal CCB actions are required and must be initiated within 30 days of this PMP Update approval:

**Action 1 — CBD-0014 Resolution:** Close the open configuration baseline deviation for cargo hook load rating. The T&E program (Phase 6B) has demonstrated 16,000 lb external load capability, providing the technical basis. A CCB action against ICD-004 is required to formally baseline the hook rating. This is a blocking item for production drawing release, supply chain qualification, and contract execution.

**Action 2 — MAAP-1C Production Scope Addition:** Formally add 15-aircraft MAAP-1C production scope to the program EVM baseline, the IMS, and the manufacturing ramp plan as a controlled scope change. The EVM Baseline Change Proposal (BCP) must be submitted within 30 days of contract execution.

**Action 3 — BOM Amendment:** Authorize the creation of Group 17, Section 17.1.5 (Cargo Variant CG-1 Equipment) in the BOM document, with drawing reference AF-MAAP1-SM-5000-00. All cargo-variant-specific items must be assigned part numbers, make/buy designations, lead time classifications, and unit costs in this BOM section before procurement actions are initiated.

**Action 4 — NRC Amortization Re-baselining:** Update Section 14.3 of the BOM to reflect the revised production base of 115 aircraft (from 100). The per-aircraft NRC burden decreases from $7,938,150 to approximately $6,903,609 — a $1,034,541 reduction per aircraft across the full program. This saving must be reflected in the program unit cost table (Section 14.4) and formally reported at the next PMR.

**Action 5 — IMS WBS Extension:** Add dedicated cargo variant development WBS elements including: cargo variant requirements and interface control, MAAP-1C tooling and fixture development, cargo-specific supplier qualification and long-lead procurement sub-schedule, cargo variant assembly flow design, and cargo-specific certification data package development.

### 4.4 Requirements Freeze and Change Control Discipline

One of the most significant margin protection mechanisms available to the program is a hard requirements freeze for the MAAP-1C configuration at contract signature. Any post-signature customer requirement changes — modifications to hook rating, cargo capacity, avionics interfaces, autonomy level, or support provisions — must be classified as Class I modifications and priced at 18–20% margin, funded by the customer. The Change Control Board must enforce this discipline without exception.

The PRB's existing framework (Section 5.1) requires Chief Engineer and PEO waiver for any deviation from the common configuration boundary. This governance process provides the contractual and technical authority to enforce the requirements freeze. The contracts team must ensure the customer agreement explicitly references the CCB process and Class I modification pricing before execution.

Any new customer requirements that deviate from the standard MAAP-1C specification — hook spacing differences, unique avionics interfaces, special cargo configurations, customer-specific software — erode the commonality metric (PRB Section 6.2) and trigger an impact assessment against all three variants per PRB Section 5.3. Commonality erosion below 80% requires formal PMR reporting and corrective action.

## 5. Cost Management Plan & Cost Baseline

### 5.1 Financial Opportunity Summary

The 15-unit MAAP-1C order represents a material financial opportunity for the program. The following table summarizes the revenue and margin scenario analysis:

| Scenario | Revenue per Unit | 15-Unit Revenue | Total Cost | Gross Margin | Margin % |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Optimistic** (FRP pricing, mature learning curve, disciplined NRE recovery) | ~$155M | $2,325M | $1,980M | $345M | 14.8% |
| **Base Case** (LRIP-era pricing, partial learning curve benefit, standard NRE) | ~$148M | $2,220M | $2,100M | $120M | 5.4% |
| **Pessimistic** (cost growth, learning curve slip, supply chain premium) | ~$148M | $2,220M | $2,225M | -$5M | -0.2% |

**The pessimistic scenario represents a loss position.** It is achievable through a combination of baseline-rate pricing, cargo variant learning curve underperformance, and supply chain cost growth — all of which are individually plausible given current program conditions. The program must not enter this contract without pricing discipline. The recommended minimum gross margin floor is **13–15%**, with 15% as the negotiating target.

### 5.2 Cargo Variant Cost Structure

A cargo variant-specific BOM does not yet exist. Until Group 17 Section CG-1 is completed, all cost estimates carry significant uncertainty. The following working estimates are established for program planning purposes, pending the Independent Cost Estimate (ICE) directed in Section 4.3:

| Cost Element | Low Estimate | High Estimate | Basis |
|---|---|---|---|
| Recurring airframe and systems (Green Aircraft baseline) | $118M | $125M per aircraft | Baseline minus armament/targeting (~$16.5M Group 12), plus cargo structural reinforcement |
| Variant-unique mission equipment (cargo systems) | $3M | $6M per aircraft | Cargo rail/roller, hooks, ramp, load monitoring |
| Program management and QA burden (estimated 12%) | $14.5M | $15.7M per aircraft | Proportional to recurring cost |
| **Estimated MAAP-1C Flyaway Cost (per aircraft)** | **~$135M** | **~$147M** | Working estimate only |
| Variant NRE (cargo delta design, tooling, qualification) | $25M | $45M | One-time; to be recovered in contract pricing |
| Supply chain re-qualification and LLI commitments | $15M | $30M | Cargo-specific supplier actions |
| Production rate disruption premium | $20M | $50M | For any schedule acceleration from FRP baseline |
| **Total Program Cost Addition (15 aircraft)** | **~$2,065M** | **~$2,275M** | Program total |

**Learning curve treatment:** The program's 85% cumulative learning curve on vertically integrated systems cannot be fully applied to the MAAP-1C. Approximately 70% of the cargo variant cost (Green Aircraft systems) inherits the existing fighter-variant learning curve and benefits from production maturity. The remaining 30% (cargo-unique systems) resets to an early-curve position for cargo-variant builds. Conservatively modeled at a 90% curve for cargo-unique systems (vs. 85% for common systems), the cost impact on 15 units is approximately $18–28M above the optimistic estimate. This risk must be priced into the contract and tracked as a standalone cost metric beginning with the first MAAP-1C production unit.

### 5.3 NRC Amortization Improvement

The increase in production base from 100 to 115 aircraft reduces the per-aircraft NRC burden from $7,938,150 to approximately $6,903,609 — a saving of approximately $1.03M per aircraft across all 115 production units. The total program NRC saving attributable to the expanded production base is approximately $15.5M (15 units × ~$1.03M per unit) in reduced amortization cost for the original 100-aircraft program. This is a genuine program-level financial benefit that partially offsets the cargo variant integration costs.

### 5.5 Cash Flow Management

The program faces a significant pre-delivery cash trough driven by long-lead procurement commitments, tooling investment, and engineering NRE — all occurring before first revenue recognition. The estimated cash profile is:

| Period | Cash Event | Estimated Impact |
|---|---|---|
| 2026–2027 | Engineering NRE, tooling orders placed | -$25–40M |
| 2027–2028 | Long-lead material commitments (engines, transmissions, cargo-unique LLI) | -$180–250M |
| 2028–2029 | Working capital — WIP for 15 additional units | -$80–120M |
| **Total pre-delivery cash trough** | | **-$285–410M** |
| 2033 onward | First delivery revenue (milestone/progress payments) | +$30–50M per unit |

To offset this cash trough, the following contract terms are required and non-negotiable:

- **Progress payments at 85% of cost incurred**, per DoD/commercial FFP program standards. Without progress payments, the program funds $285–410M of negative cash flow for 4–7 years on a fixed-price contract — an unacceptable risk to program liquidity.
- **Economic Price Adjustment (EPA) clause** covering the full delivery period. The existing program uses EPA provisions on multi-year FFP contracts. The same protection must be applied to the cargo variant contract, whose delivery window spans multiple years of inflation exposure.
- **Milestone-based payment structure** tied to production events (contract award, long-lead procurement completion, LRIP authorization, delivery acceptance) rather than calendar dates, to decouple payment triggers from regulatory schedule dependencies.

### 5.6 Management Reserve

The baseline program carries $74M in management reserve (1.5% of contract value, sized for the baseline 100-aircraft program). This reserve must not be co-mingled with the cargo variant contract's risk exposure. The following reserve structure is directed:

- **Baseline program reserve ($74M):** Maintained for baseline program risks only. Cargo variant risks are explicitly excluded from drawing on this reserve.
- **Cargo variant dedicated reserve:** A separate management reserve of $25–35M (approximately 1.5% of the $2.2B cargo contract value) must be established as a contract term requirement. The recommended approach is to require the customer to fund this reserve as part of the contract pricing, or to price it into the 13–15% gross margin floor as a contingency line item.
- **Reserve adequacy review:** The pessimistic reserve scenario for the baseline program already projects a $21M shortfall at program completion without any new risks. The Risk Manager must conduct a combined-program reserve adequacy review within 60 days of cargo contract award, incorporating the full risk register from Section 6 of this Update.

## 6. Risk Management Plan & Risk Register

### 6.1 Risk Management Approach

The cargo variant order introduces a new risk tier to the MAAP-1 program. Previously, program risks were scoped to a single-variant, single-customer production program advancing through EMD. This change adds concurrent variant production planning, a new customer relationship, a new supply chain thread, new tooling requirements, and a delivery commitment that is structurally misaligned with the current IMS. The risk management approach for this change is:

- **Risk separation:** Cargo variant risks are tracked in a dedicated risk register appendix and managed with separate mitigation funding. Baseline program risk reserve ($74M) is ring-fenced.
- **Risk authority:** The Program Director retains approval authority for any risk mitigation expenditure above $1M. Risks rated Critical (Score 16) require Program Director and CFO co-approval for mitigation funding.
- **Risk cadence:** Cargo variant risks are reviewed at the standing Monthly Program Review (PMR) under a new agenda item: *Cargo Variant Integration Status*. Critical risks receive weekly status at the Program Director's discretion.
- **Risk metrics:** Risk score = Likelihood × Consequence, 1–4 scale each, maximum Score 16. All new risks are scored in this section. Scores of 12–16 are rated High or Critical and require active mitigation plans.

### 7.3 Engine Procurement — Highest Priority Action

The engine situation requires standalone executive attention. The existing GE Aviation LTA covers exactly 40 engines through LRIP phases. Thirty additional engines are required for the 15 cargo variant aircraft (2 per aircraft). All 30 must come from GE until the second-source (Pratt & Whitney PT6T-9 derivative or equivalent) achieves production readiness, currently targeted at Q1 2030.

The GE capacity situation is acute: GE produces approximately 500 T700-series engines per year across all programs. MAAP-1 at FRP rates already consumes approximately 10% of GE output. Adding 30 engines for the cargo variant in the 2032–2034 delivery window increases MAAP-1's share of GE capacity to approximately 12–13%, at a time when GE is simultaneously managing its own $85M capacity expansion (24-month timeline, must begin Q2 2027).

Required actions, in priority order:
1. Initiate LTA amendment negotiations with GE within **30 days of program direction**. Do not wait for customer contract finalization.
2. Formally request capacity reservation for 30 additional engines in the 2032–2034 window. Budget the 3–5% capacity reservation premium.
3. Evaluate whether the second-source qualification timeline can be accelerated from Q1 2030 to Q3 2029 to provide any coverage for the latter portion of the cargo variant delivery stream.
4. Place firm orders for cargo variant engines no later than Q1 2032, regardless of contract status — the cost of holding excess inventory is significantly less than the cost of a FAL stoppage.

The following new supply chain actions are required for cargo-unique items not currently in the MAAP-1 supply chain:

Likely suppliers include Onboard Systems International, Breeze-Eastern, and specialized aerospace hook manufacturers. Qualification requires structural certification testing of the load path, release mechanism reliability testing, and cockpit interface software qualification. Estimated qualification timeline: 18–24 months from first article. Supplier identification must begin immediately upon BOM completion. Escrow tooling and data package provisions (per RISK-SC-006 framework) should be included in the supplier contract.

**Cargo Loading System (Rail/Roller, 463L-compatible):** This is a commercially available aerospace system with established suppliers including Ancra International and Pemco World Air Services. Make/Buy recommendation: **BUY** — no competitive advantage from in-house manufacture; standard BUY from lowest-cost qualified supplier. Lead time estimated at 12–16 months. Sole-source risk is low given the commercial availability of 463L systems.

**Cargo Ramp Hydraulic Actuator Assembly:** Custom-sized for the MAAP-1C clam-shell door geometry. Parker Aerospace and Eaton Aerospace are the most likely qualified sources. Make/Buy recommendation: **BUY actuators, MAKE structure.** Custom sizing requires a qualification test program. Lead time estimated at 14–18 months.

**Load Weight Indication and Proximity Alert System:** This is a sensor-software integrated system with potential single-source characteristics. The RISK-SC-006 mitigation framework (financial health monitoring, escrow tooling) must be applied. Supplier identification should begin concurrently with BOM completion.

**Titanium Primary Floor Longerons (AMS 4928 equivalent):** These structural components require titanium billet procurement, precision machining, and full NDT per ASNT SNT-TC-1A/NAS-410 protocols. Make/Buy decision depends on in-house CNC machining capability and capacity. If in-house machining is elected (the cost-efficient approach given existing aluminum machining competency), a process qualification for AMS 4928 titanium is required. BOM completion must specify geometry and tolerances before a make/buy decision can be finalized.

### 7.5 Avionics Supplier (Supplier C — Red Status) — Cargo Variant Allocation

The avionics supplier is currently at Red status with OTD of 78% and dual-source activation underway. The cargo variant must **not** increase sole-source dependency on this supplier. The cargo variant avionics orders must be allocated entirely to the emerging dual-source supplier. This serves two purposes: it reduces concentration risk, and it accelerates the dual-source supplier's production maturity through additional volume. The dual-source activation already underway should be explicitly extended to include cargo variant avionics quantities in its scope.

## 8. Quality Management Plan

### 8.1 Quality Risk from Variant Introduction

The introduction of a new variant configuration during the FRP-1/FRP-2 transition period is a well-documented quality risk in analogous programs (F-35 multi-variant production, CH-47 variant introductions). Historical data from comparable programs shows First Time Quality (FTQ) degradation of 3–8% during variant introduction periods. The program's FTQ targets — 97% (LRIP-3), 98% (FRP-1), 99% (FRP-2) — are at risk for the cargo variant production stream during its first 8–12 production units.

The quality management plan's existing "Rate Transition Management" protocol must be formally extended to cover variant introduction, not only rate increases. The protocol should include: enhanced quality surveillance for all cargo-unique assembly operations; daily production quality reviews for the first 12 cargo variant aircraft; a dedicated rapid response quality team assigned to the cargo production stream; and formal first article inspection (FAI) per AS9102 before the first cargo production unit is released to the line.

### 8.2 Cargo Variant First Article Inspection

A dedicated MAAP-1C First Article Inspection (FAI) per AS9102B is required before production units are released to the assembly line. The FAI scope must cover all cargo-variant-unique systems and interfaces, including:

- Cargo hook structural interface and load path (ICD-004 compliance verification)
- Cargo floor structure dimensional inspection (titanium longeron assembly)
- Cargo ramp mechanism kinematic verification (full cycle, hydraulic pressure)
- Cargo rail and restraint system load verification (463L compatibility)
- Cargo bay side wall and access port fit-up tolerance verification
- Weight and balance measurement (cargo configuration C.G. envelope)
- Cargo-unique wiring and avionics integration functional check

The FAI must be completed and formally closed before the second cargo unit enters the assembly line. Any FAI findings that require production drawing changes must be processed through the CCB and may impact the production drawing release milestone.

### 8.3 Special Process Qualification — Cargo-Unique Requirements

The cargo variant introduces materials and processes not currently in the Qualified Special Processes list at the scale required for production. The following special process qualifications must be completed before first cargo variant production unit:

**Titanium Structure NDT:** The cargo floor primary structure uses titanium alloy (AMS 4928 equivalent). The current NDT program covers titanium welding (AWS D17.1) and standard ASNT SNT-TC-1A/NAS-410 inspection. Titanium machined primary structure at the dimensional scale of the floor longerons requires specific ultrasonic inspection qualification that must be developed and validated. NDT procedures for the cargo hook structural attachment (10,000 lb load path) require specialized ultrasonic or radiographic inspection techniques not currently documented in the production quality plan.

**Titanium Machining Process Qualification:** If the titanium floor longerons are produced in-house (MAKE decision), the machining process for AMS 4928 at the required dimensional tolerances must be formally qualified. Titanium machining is a controlled process with specific cutting parameters, coolant requirements, and tool inspection protocols. Process qualification documentation must be completed before production.

**Cargo Ramp Hydraulic System Leak and Functional Test:** The cargo ramp hydraulic system requires a production acceptance test procedure (ATP) that does not exist in the current test flow. The test must verify full-cycle ramp operation, hydraulic pressure at rated load, leak test per MIL-STD-810, and emergency release function. A dedicated test stand is required (estimated $3–5M, included in capital plan amendment).

**Cargo Hook Load Test:** The primary cargo hook assembly requires a proof load test to rated capacity (minimum 10,000 lb threshold) on each production unit before acceptance. A structural test fixture must be designed and fabricated. The test procedure must be incorporated into the production acceptance test flow, with results documented in the aircraft acceptance data package.

### 8.4 Cargo Variant Acceptance Testing

The customer acceptance process must be extended for the MAAP-1C to include the following cargo-specific acceptance tests:

- Cargo hook proof load test (threshold: 10,000 lb; objective: 12,000 lb per KPP-03)
- Cargo ramp functional test (full cycle, hydraulic pressure, emergency release)
- Cargo rail and restraint system load verification
- External load stability augmentation software functional check (sling release, pendulum angle monitoring, load weight indication)
- Cargo weight and balance verification (C.G. at maximum cargo loading)
- Cargo-unique avionics display and crew alerting functional test

A dedicated Cargo Acceptance Test Procedure (CATP) document must be developed and approved before the first cargo production unit enters final assembly. This is a documentation critical path item whose development must begin concurrently with assembly flow design.

### 8.5 Quality Hold Policy — Cargo Variant First Articles

The baseline quality plan mandates a 90-day hold on the first three (3) production aircraft for quality assessment. This policy must be explicitly extended to the first three (3) MAAP-1C cargo production units, regardless of their position in the overall production sequence. The $2M retrofit reserve fund was sized for the baseline variant; an additional cargo-specific retrofit reserve of $1–2M should be established within the cargo variant contract management reserve to cover any rework identified during the cargo variant quality hold.

### 8.6 Supplier Quality — Cargo-Unique Items

The cargo hook assembly (10,000 lb rated) and load weight indication/proximity alert system are identified as likely sole-source or limited-source items. Per the RISK-SC-006 mitigation framework, the following supplier quality measures are required for these items:

- Resident quality inspection at the cargo hook supplier facility during first article and initial production units
- Source inspection for all hook structural components prior to shipment
- DPPM baseline established at ≤1,000 DPPM for LRIP equivalents, transitioning to ≤500 DPPM at steady-state production
- Escrow tooling and data package provisions included in supplier contracts (consistent with sole-source high-consequence item policy)
- Quarterly supplier quality audits (elevated from the standard annual cadence) for the first 18 months of production

### 8.7 Configuration Control — Quality Interface

The quality plan must be updated to reflect the cargo variant's configuration control requirements at the production level. Specifically:

- All cargo-unique drawings must carry a distinct configuration identifier (CG-1 prefix) to prevent cross-variant component substitution errors at the FAL.
- Cargo-specific work instructions must be developed as a separate, controlled document set — not as amendments to baseline variant instructions — to reduce the risk of version control errors.
- The production configuration management system must be confirmed as capable of tracking dual-variant production concurrently (baseline and cargo) without commingling build records.

## 9. Staffing & Resource Management

### 9.1 Baseline Workforce Context

The manufacturing ramp plan establishes the following approved workforce trajectory:

| Phase | Period | Workforce |
|---|---|---|
| LRIP-3 | 2028 | 450 personnel |
| FRP-1 | 2029 | 720 personnel |
| FRP-2 | 2030+ | 1,100 personnel |

The FRP-1 to FRP-2 transition identifies workforce availability as the critical path for the FRP-2 ramp. Peak hiring for FRP-2 must begin 9 months before FRP-2 activation. The current workforce plan is sized for single-variant production at the documented rates. Cargo variant production introduces new skill requirements not captured in the existing workforce plan.

### 9.2 Incremental Workforce Requirements — Cargo Variant

The following incremental workforce positions are required to support MAAP-1C production. These positions are in addition to the baseline FRP-1/FRP-2 workforce and must be incorporated into the hiring and training plan:

| Role | Quantity (Estimated) | Skill Requirements | Hiring Lead Time |
|---|---|---|---|
| Cargo systems integration technicians | 8–12 | Cargo handling equipment, hydraulic ramp assembly and rigging, sling load certification, hook installation | 9–12 months before first cargo unit |
| Titanium structure NDT inspectors | 3–5 | ASNT Level II in UT and RT; AMS 4928 inspection qualification | 12 months before first cargo unit |
| Cargo-specific quality inspectors | 4–6 | AS9102 FAI; cargo ATP procedures; hook load test operations | 9 months before first cargo unit |
| Cargo production acceptance test technicians | 2–3 | Hydraulic systems; ramp functional test; cargo hook load test | 9 months before first cargo unit |
| Cargo documentation and technical publications | 2–3 | Cargo loading manual, weight and balance manual, dangerous goods handling | 18 months before first delivery |
| Loadmaster and cargo operations training staff | 2–3 | Cargo aircraft operational training; customer acceptance support | 12 months before first delivery |
| **Total Incremental FTE — Cargo Production** | **21–32** | | |

These positions must be added to the workforce development plan (IMS WBS 4.7) and hiring initiated no later than 9–12 months before the first cargo variant unit enters production. At a projected first production entry of Q1 2033, hiring must begin no later than Q1 2032. The workforce planning milestone for cargo variant positions must be added to the IMS with appropriate predecessors.

### 9.3 Engineering Resource Requirements

The Chief Engineer has confirmed that the MAAP-1C cargo variant development requires dedicated engineering resources for the following scope items, which are not currently funded in the baseline engineering plan:

- MAAP-1C BOM delta development and release: 4–6 engineers, 60 days
- Cargo hook structural interface analysis (ICD-004 closure, CBD-0014 resolution): 2–3 structural engineers, 45 days
- Cargo ramp kinematic design and hydraulic integration: 3–4 mechanical engineers, 6 months
- Cargo floor structural reinforcement analysis (titanium longerons, AMS 4928): 2–3 structural engineers, 4 months
- Cargo variant certification data package: 3–4 engineers (structures, systems, software), 12 months
- MAAP-1C-specific production drawings release: 4–6 design engineers, integrated with cargo tooling design
- Cargo variant acceptance test procedure development: 2–3 systems engineers, 4 months

The baseline IMS caps concurrent engineering staff at 350 FTE. The cargo variant engineering scope represents an estimated 15–20 FTE increment over the peak EMD period. A resource-loaded engineering staffing plan must be developed within 30 days to confirm whether this increment can be absorbed within the 350-FTE cap or whether an authorized overage and associated cost increase is required.

### 9.4 Concurrent Engineering and Production Risk

The program is currently in EMD — the engineering team is simultaneously completing the design baseline, preparing for CDR, and supporting flight test. Adding cargo variant development engineering concurrent with these activities creates resource contention. If cargo variant engineering competes with CDR preparation for the same structural and systems engineering resources, CDR schedule risk (already a program-level concern) is amplified.

The Program Director must adjudicate engineering resource priorities within 30 days. The recommended approach is to establish a dedicated cargo variant engineering sub-team of 15–20 FTE, drawn from new hires rather than diverting existing CDR-preparation resources. This requires authorization for a net headcount increase above the 350-FTE cap, funded by the cargo variant contract.

### 9.5 Workforce Retention — Extended Program Horizon

The addition of 15 MAAP-1C aircraft extends the total production run from 350 to 365 units and pushes the final delivery approximately 1 year beyond the previously planned Q4 2034 final delivery (now approximately Q3–Q4 2035). This has two workforce management implications:

- **Positive:** Extended production horizon improves workforce retention incentive. Skilled technicians are less likely to seek employment elsewhere if the program has a confirmed multi-year production backlog extending through 2035–2036.
- **Risk:** Workforce attrition planning in the program exit strategy (IMS WBS 19) was structured around a 2034 wind-down. The extended timeline delays the start of wind-down hiring, potentially causing skills shortages if the wind-down plan is not updated to reflect the revised horizon.

The IMS exit strategy section must be updated to shift all wind-down timeline activities by approximately 12 months, with the Year -2 wind-down preparations beginning in 2034 rather than 2033.

## 10. Overall Assessment & Recommended Path Forward

### 10.1 Assessment Summary

The proposed 15-unit MAAP-1C cargo variant order is assessed as follows across the five primary program dimensions:

| Dimension | Assessment | Risk Level |
|---|---|---|
| **Revenue Opportunity** | $2.2–2.4B — material and worth pursuing aggressively | Favorable |
| **Delivery Schedule (as proposed)** | 2029 is unachievable; 48-month gap from earliest producible date; near-certain LD liability if accepted as written | **Critical** |
| **Delivery Schedule (renegotiated to 2034+)** | Achievable within the FRP-authorized production ramp; manageable with proactive supply chain action | Manageable |
| **Financial Margin** | Dilutive at baseline rates; accretive if priced at 13–15%+ gross margin; loss position in pessimistic scenario at baseline pricing | **High Risk without pricing discipline** |
| **Supply Chain** | Highest execution risk dimension; two Tier 1 suppliers already stressed; engine LTA gap is a blocking item; cargo-unique supply chain not established | **High** |
| **Manufacturing** | 15 additional units at FRP rates are within facility capacity if deliveries are phased 2034–2036; cargo variant assembly introduces new complexity requiring dedicated resources | **Medium-High** |
| **Configuration** | CBD-0014 open; MAAP-1C BOM nonexistent; both are blocking items for procurement and pricing | **High** |
| **Quality** | Variant introduction during production ramp is a known quality risk; manageable with dedicated FAI, cargo CATP, and special process qualification | **Medium** |
| **Workforce** | 21–32 incremental FTE required; timeline permits orderly hiring if initiated by Q1 2032; engineering resource contention with CDR is an immediate concern | **Medium** |

**Overall Order Assessment: ACCEPTABLE — with conditions.** The order is financially attractive, commercially strategic, and technically feasible within the program's existing architecture. It must not be accepted as proposed. It must be accepted on the terms specified in Sections 5.7 and 10.2 of this Update.

### 10.6 Executive Decision Required

The Program Director and CFO are directed to jointly review this PMP Update and provide a disposition decision within 14 days of issuance. The disposition options are:

**Option A — Proceed with conditions:** Authorize the program to begin pre-contractual actions (Sections 10.3 and 10.4) and enter delivery schedule and pricing negotiations with the new customer on the basis of the terms in Section 10.5. Contract execution remains contingent on all pre-conditions in Section 10.2 being satisfied.

**Option B — Defer pending BOM completion:** Authorize pre-contractual supply chain engagement (GE LTA amendment, avionics dual-source extension, prepreg allocation renegotiation) but defer contract negotiations until the MAAP-1C BOM delta is complete and the ICE is finalized. This option reduces financial risk from premature pricing commitments but delays revenue recognition by approximately 60–90 days.

**Option C — Decline:** Determine that the program cannot accommodate this order within the current resource and schedule environment without unacceptable risk to the baseline program. This option is not recommended — the financial upside and strategic value of the order are sufficient to justify pursuing Option A or B — but it is documented as a valid executive decision available if the risk posture assessment leads to this conclusion.

**Recommended Decision: Option A.** The order is worth pursuing. The supply chain actions in Section 10.3 cannot wait for BOM completion or ICE finalization — the engine lead time situation is urgent enough to require immediate engagement with GE regardless of ultimate contract disposition. Proceeding with pre-contractual actions now is the most effective risk management posture, provided all pre-conditions in Section 10.2 are satisfied before binding commitments are made to the customer.
